points.text: Collect timestamps for request start, DB query start/end, and response send to pinpoint where latency occurs.
Enable DB slow‑query logging (e.g., MySQL slow_query_log) to capture queries exceeding a threshold.
Run network latency tests (ping, traceroute) from the app server to the DB server during slow periods.
Capture packets (tcpdump/wireshark) on the DB connection to measure retransmissions or loss.
Compare average DB execution time vs. total request time; if DB time dominates, focus on database tuning.
If network RTT spikes align with slow requests, suspect network congestion or packet loss as the cause.
Quick fix: increase DB connection pool size or enable connection reuse to reduce handshake overhead.
Quick fix: add missing indexes or rewrite identified slow SQL queries to improve execution speed.
Capture query latency with pg_stat_statements (or equivalent) to pinpoint which SQL statements are slow.
Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on identified slow queries to detect missing indexes or suboptimal join order.
Monitor DB server CPU, memory, and disk I/O during performance spikes to rule out resource saturation.
Check connection pool size and wait queue; adjust pool limits if connections are queuing.
Ping and traceroute from the application host to the DB server to detect latency or packet loss.
If packet loss is observed, inspect NICs, switches, or VPNs; consider relocating the DB closer to the app.
Quick DB fix: create or refine indexes suggested by the EXPLAIN output to speed up scans.
Quick DB fix: tune work_mem, shared_buffers, or isolation level to reduce contention.
Collect baseline latency: ping the endpoint and measure RTT and packet loss over a 5‑minute window.
Run a continuous TCP trace (tcpdump) on the server to observe retransmissions and high RTT spikes.
Check OS network stats (netstat -s, ss) for drops, errors, and TCP retransmit counters.
Correlate timestamps: align periods of packet loss with entries in the DB slow‑query log.
Enable the DB slow‑query log; identify queries exceeding a latency threshold and examine their plans.
Execute the same query locally on the DB host to see if latency persists without network hop.
Temporarily SSH into the DB server and run the query; fast results indicate network as the cause.
Quick network fix: restart the NIC, verify MTU, enable TCP window scaling, or increase socket buffers.
OUT_OF_SCOPE: The question pertains to system performance troubleshooting, which is outside the math_logic domain.
UNKNOWN: Unable to determine a solution within the math_logic scope.
OUT_OF_SCOPE: The request asks about performance isolation between DB and network, which is outside defensive security scope.
UNKNOWN: No security-relevant information is provided to evaluate authentication, session safety, or secure coding aspects.
